1: David’s declaration: “This is to be the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of burnt offering for Israel.” The demonstrative zeh (this) is emphatic — the specific threshing floor is named as the permanent cultic site. The Chronicler understands this declaration as the direct consequence of the plague-altar sequence: David’s recognition that YHWH has designated this place. 2 Chronicles 3:1 later confirms it as “Mount Moriah” — linking the temple to the Abrahamic sacrifice site (Genesis 22:2).
2: David assembles the gērîm — the foreign residents (gēr, H1616 = a resident alien with protected but non-citizen status) — as stonecutters. The use of resident aliens for skilled construction labor reflects both ANE practice and Israelite social law: the gēr had legal protections (Exodus 23:9; Deuteronomy 24:17-18) but was subject to conscription for state labor projects (cf. Solomon’s later forced levy of all Israel, 1 Kings 5:13-18). The Solomonic temple was built with significant foreign labor.
3: Iron for nails and bronze in vast quantities — David’s iron procurement is significant because iron was still a relatively controlled material in early Iron Age Canaan. The Philistines had maintained a near-monopoly on iron smithing (1 Samuel 13:19-22); David’s campaigns against Philistia broke this monopoly. The Chronicler’s emphasis on David stockpiling iron reflects the historical reality that the Iron Age transitioned partly through Davidic military success.
4: Cedar from the Sidonians and Tyrians — the Lebanon cedar trade was one of the most significant resource relationships in the ANE. Lebanon cedar (ʾerez hallĕḇānôn) was prized throughout the Near East for its durability, straight grain, and fragrance; it was used in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant for palace and temple construction. The Phoenician trading cities of Sidon and Tyre controlled the timber trade as their primary economic resource. David’s establishment of this supply relationship is the pre-condition for Solomon’s treaty with Hiram of Tyre (1 Kings 5:1-12).
5: David’s self-assessment: “My son Solomon is young (naʿar) and inexperienced (raḵ).” The Hebrew naʿar covers a wide range (infant to young adult) but here emphasizes inexperience with statecraft; raḵ (tender, inexperienced) suggests both youth and the lack of hardening that comes from years of war and governance. David’s logic: the temple project requires preparation precisely because Solomon will not yet have the resources, relationships, or experience to acquire them independently.
6: David’s charge to Solomon begins here — a private father-to-son commissioning speech that has no parallel in 1-2 Kings. The Chronicler develops this as a theological centrepiece: before his death, David gives Solomon the theological rationale for the temple, the divine word disqualifying David, and the materials inventory. The private charge sets up the public charge in 1 Chronicles 28-29. ANE royal inscriptions often include the deceased king’s instructions for temple maintenance as a form of legitimation for the successor.
7: “It was my deepest desire (ʿim lĕḇāḇî — “with my heart”) to build a house for the name of the LORD.” The phrase “for the name of the LORD” (lĕšēm YHWH) is Deuteronomic theology: YHWH does not literally dwell in the temple but his šēm (name = presence, authority, character) is placed there (Deuteronomy 12:5, 11; 1 Kings 8:29). The “name” theology guards against crude identification of YHWH with a localized deity — he is present in the temple representatively, not spatially confined.
8: “You have shed much blood (dāmîm rabbîm) and fought many wars — you are not to build a house for my name.” The blood-guilt principle: a builder of the LORD’s house cannot be a man of blood. The concern is not merely ritual impurity (blood as defiling) but symbolic-theological fitness — the house of the God of peace requires a man of peace as its builder. The ANE parallel: Gudea of Lagash’s temple-building inscriptions emphasize the builder’s ritual purity and divine election; the wrong kind of person building a temple was a theological problem.
9: Solomon’s name (Šĕlōmōh) is connected etymologically to šālôm (peace) — “his name will be Solomon (Peace), and I will give Israel peace and quiet during his reign.” The naming theology is explicit: the builder’s identity must match the character of what he builds. ANE temple-builders were understood as bringing their character to the sacred space — the god who accepted the temple accepted the builder. Solomon’s “rest” (mĕnûḥâ) is the condition YHWH promised for life in the land (Deuteronomy 12:10-11).
10: “He will be my son and I will be his father” — the adoption formula from the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:14; Psalms 2:7). In ANE royal ideology, the king was understood as the god’s son in a functional sense — representing the deity on earth, executing divine will, maintaining divine order. The Israelite version of this is covenantal rather than ontological: Solomon is YHWH’s son by covenant relationship, not by divine generation. David transmits this covenant promise directly to Solomon, making the chain of Davidic covenant continuity explicit.
11: The blessing formula “May the LORD be with you” (YHWH ʿimmāḵ) is the standard covenantal accompaniment formula — the same blessing given to Joshua (Joshua 1:5; Deuteronomy 31:8) and to Gideon (Judges 6:12). David is passing on both a task and a covenantal relationship. The success of the temple project depends not on Solomon’s skill or resources alone but on YHWH’s active presence. The formula grounds the entire enterprise in relational theology rather than royal achievement.
12: “May the LORD give you wisdom (śekel) and discernment (ûḇînâ) when he puts you in charge of Israel.” The prayer for wisdom echoes the later Solomonic tradition of 1 Kings 3:5-14 (Solomon’s dream at Gibeon) and Proverbs 1:1-7 (where Solomon is the patron of wisdom). Śekel (prudence, insight) and bînâ (understanding, discernment) together represent practical and theoretical wisdom — both needed for governing and for the complex management of a temple construction project.
13: “You will succeed if you carefully follow the statutes and ordinances the LORD commanded Moses for Israel.” The conditional success formula echoes the Joshua commissioning (Joshua 1:7-8) almost verbatim — the same book of the law, the same requirement to observe it carefully. David frames Solomon’s kingship in the Deuteronomic mold: royal success is not a function of military or administrative genius but of covenant faithfulness. The Torah is the charter for the Davidic monarchy.
14: “In my distress (bĕʿōnî) I have prepared one hundred thousand talents of gold, a million talents of silver...” The quantities are hyperbolic in the extreme — 100,000 talents of gold would be approximately 3,750 tons; the total silver would be far larger. ANE royal inscriptions routinely use hyperbolic quantities to express magnificence and generosity: Assyrian annals record tribute and tribute-lists in inflated terms. The Chronicler’s point is theological: David’s preparation was proportionate to the grandeur of what YHWH deserves, not to any literal accounting.
15: The workforce inventory — stonecutters, masons, carpenters, craftsmen in every material. The Chronicler’s David is presented as the Moses of the temple construction: just as Moses was given skilled artisans filled with the Spirit of God for the tabernacle (Exodus 31:1-11; 1 Chronicles 35:30-36:1), David assembles and prepares a comparable workforce for the temple. The template for sacred construction is always: divine design, human craftsmanship, divine enabling.
16: “Arise and get to work! The LORD be with you.” The final charge is a commissioning formula combining command and blessing — the same structure as Moses’s charge to the assembly before the tabernacle construction (Exodus 36:2) and YHWH’s charge to Joshua (Joshua 1:6-7). The work of building the divine dwelling is not human initiative but divine commission executed through human obedience. David transmits this covenantal framework intact to Solomon.
17: David charges the leaders of Israel to support Solomon — the collective leadership structure of the Chronicler’s David. Unlike the Samuel narrative where David rules more individually, the Chronicler consistently presents a David who builds coalitions and delegates: the Levitical appointments, the military commanders, the tribal leaders. The call to “support” (ʿizrû, from ʿāzar = to help, support) Solomon is both political (loyalty pledge) and practical (resource provision).
18: “Is not the LORD your God with you? Has he not given you rest on every side?” David’s rhetorical argument to the leaders invokes the accomplished military rest as the theological condition for temple-building. The ANE connection: temple construction was a royal prerogative exercised in a time of peace — you could not build a divine house while fighting wars. The rest David has achieved through military campaigns is now the theological platform for the building project. The inhabitants of the land have been “handed over” (nittĕnâ hāʾāreṣ) — language of covenant gift.
19: “Devote your whole heart and soul (nĕpešḵem) to seeking (liḏrōš) the LORD your God.” The Deuteronomic phrase “with all your heart and soul” (Deuteronomy 6:5; 1 Chronicles 10:12; 1 Chronicles 11:13; 1 Chronicles 26:16) represents the covenant ideal of total orientation toward YHWH. The verb dāraš (to seek, inquire of) is specifically the vocabulary of cultic inquiry and devoted pursuit of YHWH — the opposite of seeking the Baals (2 Chronicles 17:3-4). The construction of the sanctuary is itself an act of “seeking” — making space for the divine presence in the midst of the people.
